title: El Niño Is Already Wreaking Havoc on Pacific Fisheries
gist: Less than a month into a 'super' El Niño, Pacific fisheries are already diverging sharply: Peru has canceled its anchovy season and India expects smaller mackerel catches, while Southern California is seeing record tuna hauls, with knock-on effects for global fish-oil, animal-feed, and seafood prices.

themes.theme: Divergent winners and losers across fisheries

insights.claim: Peru's government has effectively canceled the anchovy fishing season, an export critical to global fish-oil and animal-feed supply, due to the current 'super' El Niño.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: A canceled Peruvian anchovy season directly constrains global fishmeal/fish-oil supply, a key input for aquaculture and animal-feed industries, implying upward price pressure.
insights.locator: In Peru, government officials have effectively canceled

insights.claim: The Indian government is preparing for a season of smaller, less plentiful Indian mackerel catches due to El Niño.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Signals regional food-security and price pressure in South Asian seafood markets tied to the same global climate driver.
insights.locator: The Indian government is preparing for a season

insights.claim: Southern California recreational and commercial fishers reported some of the most successful months of tuna fishing they have ever seen, even before El Niño was officially declared in June.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Regional fishing economies can benefit sharply from the same climate event that devastates others, creating investable divergence within the seafood supply chain.
insights.locator: in Southern California, recreational and commercial fishers

insights.claim: One fishing tracker suggests nearly 300,000 more bluefin tuna were caught off the California coast in the first half of the year compared to the same period last year.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: A concrete, large-magnitude catch increase quantifies the regional upside from El Niño for California's fishing economy.
insights.locator: one fishing tracker suggests that nearly 300,000 more

insights.claim: El Niño creates winners and losers across the fishing industry by decimating some species while making others easier to catch, forcing fishers toward seasonal diversification and creating price instability for consumers.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Frames El Niño as a structural volatility driver for global seafood supply chains and pricing, relevant to commodity and agriculture-adjacent investment theses.
insights.locator: The divergent situations show how El Niño can create winners and losers

insights.claim: Under normal conditions, trade winds drive upwelling of cold, nutrient-dense water that feeds algae growth; during El Niño, weakening trade winds slow or stop this upwelling, pushing algae-dependent species like anchovies into deeper, harder-to-reach waters and stressing their populations.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Explains the physical mechanism behind fishery disruption, useful for forecasting which species/regions are structurally exposed to future El Niño cycles.
insights.locator: The reason it has such disparate impacts

insights.claim: Peruvian fishing adviser Humberto Speziani said sonar-equipped vessels have located anchovies more than 100 meters below the surface, twice the depth reachable with normal purse seine fishing nets.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Quantifies the physical inaccessibility of anchovy stocks during this El Niño, supporting the case for a prolonged supply disruption rather than a temporary dip.
insights.locator: Humberto Speziani, a Peruvian industrial fishing adviser

insights.claim: El Niño often pushes warm-water species like skipjack tuna into coastal waters of the Americas that are normally too cold for them, making these species easier to catch nearer shore.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific mechanism behind the tuna/shrimp catch boom, useful for anticipating which fisheries benefit in future El Niño events.
insights.locator: El Niño often sees warm-water species

insights.claim: Wild salmon can become so thin from food scarcity during El Niño that they are nicknamed 'snakes,' and their decline in North American coastal waters can raise dock prices that pass through to retail and restaurant customers.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Direct link from El Niño biology to consumer seafood price inflation, relevant for food-cost forecasting.
insights.locator: Wild salmon, for example, can get so skinny

insights.claim: In local Peruvian markets, prices for jack mackerel and corvina have already reportedly doubled, prompting families to shift toward buying more chicken instead.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: A concrete doubling of local fish prices with visible substitution to poultry is an early, measurable inflation and consumer-behavior signal from this El Niño.
insights.locator: prices for jack mackerel and corvina have already reportedly doubled

insights.claim: Fisheries economist Juan Carlos Sueiro said shrimp populations, unlike anchovies, tend to boom during El Niño, so shrimp prices may move in the opposite direction from other fish.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Suggests a divergent commodity trade within seafood itself: short anchovy/fishmeal supply, long shrimp supply, during the same climate event.
insights.locator: Sueiro said the opposite may happen with species like shrimp

insights.claim: Research suggests that El Niño-driven shifts in fish migration can escalate geopolitical conflict as fishing vessels stray into other countries' economic zones.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: A non-obvious geopolitical tail risk from a climate/biological event, relevant to monitoring maritime disputes in affected regions.
insights.locator: there’s been some research to suggest that shifting fish populations

insights.claim: Scientist Arnaud Bertrand warns that if the Humboldt squid fishery collapses, roughly 10,000 Peruvian artisanal fishing boats would seek alternative resources, potentially causing major ecosystem damage since artisanal fishers are less regulated than commercial operations.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific cascading risk scenario (squid collapse to fleet displacement to ecosystem damage) with a concrete fleet-size figure for scale.
insights.quote: If the Humboldt squid collapses, then you’ll have 10,000 boats that will try to find another resource

insights.claim: The Humboldt squid fishery yields half a million tons of catch per year and is an important income source for Peru's artisanal fishers.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of a fishery at risk, providing a baseline for estimating potential economic loss if the squid population collapses.
insights.locator: they yield half a million tons of catch per year

insights.claim: High ocean temperatures from El Niño can decimate coral reefs, accelerate kelp deterioration, and reduce underwater oxygen needed to sustain healthy marine ecosystems.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Broadens the impact assessment beyond fisheries economics to long-term marine ecosystem degradation.
insights.locator: High ocean temperatures associated with the weather phenomenon

insights.claim: Oceana Peru economist Juan Carlos Sueiro says climate change is expected to drive more frequent, stronger El Niños, increasing fishing communities' vulnerability over time.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Positions the current disruption as part of a worsening long-term trend rather than a one-off event, relevant to long-horizon climate-risk assessment for seafood supply chains.
insights.locator: As climate change is expected to drive more frequent

insights.claim: Exceptionally high temperatures in September could signal a more damaging El Niño on par with the disastrous 1982 event, though scientist Arnaud Bertrand notes each El Niño is different and its full impact is hard to predict in advance.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete forward-looking checkpoint (September temperature readings) to watch for signs of a historically severe El Niño.
insights.locator: Ultimately, the exact impacts will depend

insights.claim: Bertrand says that with global warming, 'the worst is the most probable' outcome for future El Niño severity.
insights.speaker: Joseph Winters
insights.so_what: A pessimistic expert framing that argues against assuming El Niño impacts will moderate over time, useful for tail-risk modeling in climate-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: With global warming, the worst is the most probable.
insights.locator: though climate change doesn’t make him optimistic
